preFix.title: Needle-felted landscape picture
preFix.subtitle: A flat needle-felted landscape panel, approximately 25 × 20 cm, in merino on a wool felt background
preFix.excerpt: A flat needle-felted landscape panel built up in layers on a firm wool felt background. Distant hills are laid first in muted blues and greys, then a mid-ground of fields in greens and ochres, then a foreground of grass and hedgerow detail. The finished panel can be mounted in a frame.
preFix.sourceNotes: Flat needle-felted landscape panels are a common introductory needle-felting project in UK craft classes. Technique synthesised from general needle-felted picture work.

attrs.body: Always stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. The needle must enter foam, not a hard surface, on every stroke.
attrs.title: Needle safety

content.text: A flat needle-felted landscape is built in layers from background to foreground, following the principle of
atmospheric perspective
: distant elements in pale, muted tones, closer elements in stronger, more saturated colour. The
roving
is stabbed into a pre-made wool felt background using a
barbed needle

. Each new layer bonds over and blends into the previous one, building depth. The sky is usually incorporated into the background felt colour rather than added separately, which keeps the horizon clean.

items.name: Wool felt background panel, 30 × 25 cm, sky blue or pale grey
Merino roving, ochre-yellow (harvest fields or moorland)
Merino roving, darker green (hedgerows, foreground trees)
Merino roving, brown (soil, paths, bare earth)
Foam felting mat, at least 30 × 25 cm

content.text: Method
Step 1: Plan the composition

Lay the background felt on the felting mat. Sketch the horizon line lightly with a chalk marker or simply hold the composition in mind. A strong landscape composition has a low horizon (one-third of the way up from the bottom of the panel), a clear distinction between the sky area (which uses the background felt colour), a distant hill band, a mid-ground field area, and a foreground with more detail.
Step 2: Work the distant hills

Pull thin wisps of pale blue-grey roving and lay them across the background just below the horizon line, forming gentle rounded shapes. Use a size 38 needle to stab the roving into the background. Keep the edges soft by using light, infrequent stabs along the boundary between hill and sky: a hard edge reads as close rather than distant.
Step 3: Build the mid-ground fields

Lay bands of mid green and ochre-yellow roving across the lower half of the panel, overlapping the base of the distant hills slightly. Vary the field widths and alternate the colours to create a patchwork effect. Keep the edges between fields crisp by stabbing more firmly along each boundary. A slightly darker green stabbed along the field boundaries suggests hedgerow lines.
Step 4: Add foreground detail

Work the foreground using stronger, more saturated greens and browns. Short vertical wisps of darker green stabbed close together suggest grass and rough vegetation. Small masses of darker roving form trees or hedgerow clumps. Use a size 40 needle for fine detail in the foreground: short stabbing strokes build texture without flattening the roving into a solid mass.
Step 5: Add sky elements and finishing detail

Add small pulled wisps of white roving in the upper part of the panel for clouds: stab lightly to keep them fluffy rather than flat. A few tiny white spots in the mid-ground suggest distant sheep. Step back from the panel regularly to check the overall tonal balance. Anything that reads as too flat or too uniform can have a few tufts of a lighter or darker shade stabbed over it to add depth.

items.fix: Add very pale, almost white wisps of roving over the distant hills to push them further back. Increase the richness and variety of colour in the foreground and middle ground.
items.cause: All layers were worked in similar saturation and the size distinction between near and far elements was not maintained
items.problem: The landscape looks flat with no sense of depth

items.fix: Add a few wisps of the background colour over the top edge of the hills and stab lightly to blend the boundary. The horizon should dissolve gradually into the sky colour.
items.cause: The distant hill roving was stabbed too firmly along its upper edge
items.problem: The sky-to-hill boundary has a hard line rather than a soft horizon

items.fix: Add thin additional wisps of the appropriate colour over the thin patches and stab to integrate. Build up coverage gradually rather than using thick clumps.
items.cause: The roving layers were too thin in those areas
items.problem: The background felt shows through the middle ground in patches

content.text: Finishing and framing

Trim the panel to size with scissors if the background felt edges are uneven. The panel can be mounted on a piece of foam board with a thin layer of fabric glue and framed under glass. Alternatively, stretch the panel on an embroidery hoop or attach small dowels top and bottom for a hanging display. A soft brush can fluff any areas that have become too compacted during working.

initialFindings.snippet: A flat needle-felted landscape is built in layers from background to foreground, following the principle of atmospheric perspective: distant
